frontier.name: BBB Flagship
frontier.description: Bounded flagship frontier for BBB delivery strategies for Alzheimer's-relevant therapeutics. Curated from checked-in abstract-level source files under projects/bbb-flagship/raw.

assertion.text: The Brain Shuttle version of an anti-Aβ antibody, utilizing a monovalent binding mode to the transferrin receptor (TfR), increases β-Amyloid target engagement in an Alzheimer's disease mouse model by 55-fold compared to the parent antibody.

evidence_spans.text: Brain Shuttle version of an anti-Aβ antibody, which uses a monovalent binding mode to the TfR, increases β-Amyloid target engagement in a mouse model of Alzheimer's disease by 55-fold compared to the parent antibody.

conditions.text: Monovalent Brain Shuttle module engineered into an anti-Aβ antibody.

provenance.title: Increased brain penetration and potency of a therapeutic antibody using a monovalent molecular shuttle.

assertion.text: Abeta interaction with RAGE-bearing cells in the vessel wall results in the expression of proinflammatory cytokines.

entities.name: RAGE
aliases: receptor for advanced glycation end products

evidence.method: Systemic Abeta infusion and genetic manipulation

evidence_spans.text: Systemic Abeta infusion and studies in genetically manipulated mice show that Abeta interaction with receptor for advanced glycation end products (RAGE)-bearing cells in the vessel wall results in transport of Abeta across the blood-brain barrier (BBB) and expression of proinflammatory cytokines and endothelin-1 (ET-1), the latter mediating Abeta-induced vasoconstriction.

conditions.text: Systemic Abeta infusion in genetically manipulated mice.

confidence.basis: frontier_epistemic: evidence=0.80 * replication=0.70 * model=0.80 * sample=0.60 - review_penalty=0.00 + calibration=0.00 = 0.269

provenance.title: RAGE mediates amyloid-beta peptide transport across the blood-brain barrier and accumulation in brain.

assertion.text: Amyloid beta-peptide amyloid-beta 1-40 binds to immobilized LRP clusters II and IV with high affinity, with a dissociation constant (Kd) ranging from 0.6 to 1.2 nM.

evidence_spans.text: Here, we report amyloid beta-peptide Abeta40 binds to immobilized LRP clusters II and IV with high affinity (Kd = 0.6-1.2 nM) compared to Abeta42 and mutant Abeta, and LRP-mediated Abeta brain capillary binding, endocytosis, and transcytosis across the mouse blood-brain barrier are substantially reduced by the high beta sheet content in Abeta and deletion of the receptor-associated protein gene.

conditions.text: immobilized LRP clusters II and IV

assertion.text: The balance between RAGE and LRP1 at the blood-brain barrier regulates the clearance and transport of amyloid beta-peptide across the barrier.

entities.name: RAGE
aliases: receptor for advanced glycation end products

evidence_spans.text: In this article, we summarize recent evidence why the receptor for advanced glycation end products and low-density lipoprotein receptor related protein 1 in the vascular CNS barriers are critical for regulation of Abeta homeostasis in the CNS and how altered activities in these 2 receptors at the blood-brain barrier may contribute to the CNS Abeta accumulation resulting in neuroinflammation, disconnect between the cerebral blood flow and metabolism, altered synaptic transmission, neuronal injury, and amyloid deposition into parenchymal and neurovascular lesions.

conditions.text: Vascular CNS barriers in animal models of Alzheimer's disease.

confidence.basis: frontier_epistemic: evidence=0.80 * replication=0.70 * model=0.80 * sample=0.60 - review_penalty=0.00 + calibration=0.00 = 0.269

conditions.text: six monthly aducanumab infusions combined with focused ultrasound treatment in selected brain regions over 6 months

guided Aβ trafficking to Rab5 and Rab11, leading to Aβ endothelial transcytosis and clearance.
the bispecific antibody accumulated in the mouse brain and led to a greater reduction in brain Aβ after a single systemic dose.

Systemic Abeta infusion and studies in genetically manipulated mice show that Abeta interaction with receptor for advanced glycation end products (RAGE)-bearing cells in the vessel wall results in transport of Abeta across the blood-brain barrier (BBB) and expression of proinflammatory cytokines and endothelin-1 (ET-1), the latter mediating Abeta-induced vasoconstriction.

Here, we report amyloid beta-peptide Abeta40 binds to immobilized LRP clusters II and IV with high affinity (Kd = 0.6-1.2 nM) compared to Abeta42 and mutant Abeta, and LRP-mediated Abeta brain capillary binding, endocytosis, and transcytosis across the mouse blood-brain barrier are substantially reduced by the high beta sheet content in Abeta and deletion of the receptor-associated protein gene.
how altered activities in these 2 receptors at the blood-brain barrier may contribute to the CNS Abeta accumulation

Anti-TfR antibodies that bind with high affinity to TfR remain associated with the BBB, whereas lower-affinity anti-TfR antibody variants are released from the BBB into the brain and show a broad distribution 24 hours after dosing.

LRP-mediated Abeta brain capillary binding, endocytosis, and transcytosis across the mouse blood-brain barrier are substantially reduced by the high beta sheet content in Abeta and deletion of the receptor-associated protein gene.
Exploratory analysis suggested no group-wise changes in amyloid post-sonication.
Intravenous dosing of monkeys with anti-TfR/BACE1 antibodies also reduced Aβ both in cerebral spinal fluid and in brain tissue
reduce brain Aβ in a TfR affinity-dependent fashion.
it promotes proteasome-dependent LRP degradation in endothelium at concentrations > 1 microM

Inhibition of RAGE-ligand interaction suppresses accumulation of Abeta in brain parenchyma in a mouse transgenic model.

potential of advanced glycation end products and low-density lipoprotein receptor related protein 1-based therapeutic strategies to control brain Abeta in animal models of AD
Single-cell RNA sequencing and morphometry revealed that ATV:TREM2 shifted microglia to metabolically responsive states, which were distinct from those induced by amyloid pathology.
These results demonstrate that the TfR bispecific antibody platform can robustly and safely deliver therapeutic antibody across the BBB in the primate brain.
In an AD mouse model, ATV:TREM2 boosted brain microglial activity and glucose metabolism.

In this article, we summarize recent evidence why the receptor for advanced glycation end products and low-density lipoprotein receptor related protein 1 in the vascular CNS barriers are critical for regulation of Abeta homeostasis in the CNS and how altered activities in these 2 receptors at the blood-brain barrier may contribute to the CNS Abeta accumulation resulting in neuroinflammation, disconnect between the cerebral blood flow and metabolism, altered synaptic transmission, neuronal injury, and amyloid deposition into parenchymal and neurovascular lesions.

Brain Shuttle version of an anti-Aβ antibody, which uses a monovalent binding mode to the TfR, increases β-Amyloid target engagement in a mouse model of Alzheimer's disease by 55-fold compared to the parent antibody.
Opening the blood-brain barrier did not result in serious clinical or radiographic adverse events
Whether Abeta-related pathology in the CNS is reversible or not and what key therapeutic targets are controlling Abeta/amyloid levels in the aging brain remain debatable.
PICALM levels and Aβ clearance were reduced in AD-derived endothelial monolayers, which was reversible by adenoviral-mediated PICALM transfer.
reduced brain capillary LRP levels in Abeta-accumulating transgenic mice, AD, and patients with cerebrovascular beta-amyloidosis.

reducing the affinity of an antibody for the transferrin receptor (TfR) enhances receptor-mediated transcytosis of the anti-TfR antibody across the BBB into the mouse brain where it reaches therapeutically relevant concentrations.
no clinically significant worsening on cognitive scores at three months compared to baseline.
In human induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived microglia, ATV:TREM2 induced proliferation and improved mitochondrial metabolism.
PICALM deficiency diminished Aβ clearance across the murine blood-brain barrier (BBB)
endothelin-1 (ET-1), the latter mediating Abeta-induced vasoconstriction.
accelerated Aβ pathology in a manner that was reversible by endothelial PICALM re-expression.
We provide in vitro and in vivo evidence that the monovalent binding mode facilitates transcellular transport, whereas a bivalent binding mode leads to lysosome sorting.

Here, we report amyloid beta-peptide Abeta40 binds to immobilized LRP clusters II and IV with high affinity (Kd = 0.6-1.2 nM) compared to Abeta42 and mutant Abeta, and LRP-mediated Abeta brain capillary binding, endocytosis, and transcytosis across the mouse blood-brain barrier are substantially reduced by the high beta sheet content in Abeta and deletion of the receptor-associated protein gene.
